thesis: The tool is never neutral. Every design choice carries values into the world.
framing: Each card pairs a tool with the specific design decision its makers made, and the real-world consequence that decision produced. No bias-laundering. Name the choice. Name what it did.

cases.decision: Trained and benchmarked on datasets dominated by lighter-skinned male faces; shipped without disaggregated accuracy reporting.
cases.outcome: Misidentified darker-skinned women at a 34.7% error rate vs. 0.8% for lighter-skinned men — a 40x gap baked into infrastructure governments and police were already buying.
cases.harm: racism and sexism embedded in code
cases.preciseName: compounded racial and gender discrimination, with darker-skinned women carrying the worst error rate by orders of magnitude
cases.citation: Buolamwini, J. & Gebru, T. (2018). Gender Shades. Proceedings of Machine Learning Research 81.

cases.decision: Trained on 10 years of Amazon's own hiring decisions and let the model infer what a 'good candidate' looked like.
cases.outcome: The model learned to penalize the word 'women's' (as in 'women's chess club captain') and downrank graduates of all-women's colleges. Amazon scrapped it before deployment — but only after building it.
cases.harm: automated devaluation of women's resumes — a feedback loop that would have made the historical pattern self-reinforcing
cases.preciseName: encoded historical hiring discrimination, automated and scaled, with the side effect of laundering the discrimination as 'objective' machine output
cases.citation: Dastin, J. (2018). 'Amazon scraps secret AI recruiting tool that showed bias against women.' Reuters.

cases.decision: Optimized a single overall accuracy number; never required calibrated false-positive parity across racial groups before selling to courts.
cases.outcome: Black defendants who didn't re-offend were flagged high-risk at 45%; white defendants in the same situation, 23%. Judges used those scores to set bail and sentencing.
cases.harm: racially disparate over-policing dressed up as actuarial science
cases.preciseName: the system mislabels Black defendants as high-risk at twice the false-positive rate of white defendants, and the courts use those scores to set bail and sentencing
cases.citation: Angwin, J., Larson, J., Mattu, S., & Kirchner, L. (2016). 'Machine Bias.' ProPublica.

cases.tool: A widely-deployed US hospital risk-prediction algorithm (Optum / used on ~200M patients)
cases.decision: Picked predicted future health-care cost as the proxy for medical need — because cost data was easy to get.
cases.outcome: Black patients had to be significantly sicker than white patients to receive the same risk score, because the system had historically spent less on them. The proxy laundered structural under-spending as 'they need less care.'
cases.harm: structural under-spending on Black patients was being laundered as 'they need less care' by the algorithm
cases.preciseName: the algorithm optimized for predicted cost, which encoded historical under-spending on Black patients, and recommended care allocation as if cost equalled need
cases.citation: Obermeyer, Z., Powers, B., Vogeli, C., & Mullainathan, S. (2019). 'Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations.' Science.

cases.tool: Hate-speech classifiers licensed to social platforms
cases.decision: Trained on annotator-labeled toxicity datasets without correcting for annotators' unfamiliarity with African American English.
cases.outcome: AAVE tweets were flagged toxic at 1.5x to 2.2x the rate of equivalent standard-English tweets. Black users were silenced more often by the same 'neutral' moderation tool.
cases.harm: differential censorship of Black users' speech, embedded in the moderation infrastructure of every platform that licensed those classifiers
cases.preciseName: platforms silence Black users' speech at twice the rate of equivalent white speech, while branding the system as objective
cases.citation: Sap, M., Card, D., Gabriel, S., Choi, Y., & Smith, N. A. (2019). 'The Risk of Racial Bias in Hate Speech Detection.' ACL 2019.

cases.tool: Top-five voice-assistant speech recognizers (Apple, Amazon, Google, IBM, Microsoft)
cases.decision: Trained acoustic models on a corpus dominated by white General-American speech; treated that distribution as 'standard.'
cases.outcome: Word-error rate for Black speakers was nearly twice that of white speakers — every voice phone-tree, dictation tool, and in-car system silently worked worse for them.
cases.harm: voice interfaces silently work worse for Black speakers across every product that licenses these recognizers
cases.preciseName: the acoustic model was trained on a corpus dominated by white General-American speech, so every speaker outside that distribution gets misheard at scale
cases.citation: Koenecke, A. et al. (2020). 'Racial disparities in automated speech recognition.' PNAS.

cases.tool: PredPol-style predictive-policing systems (LAPD, Chicago, dozens more)
cases.decision: Used past arrest locations as the input signal — treating arrest data as if it were crime data.
cases.outcome: Patrols were sent back to neighborhoods that were already over-policed, generating more arrests, feeding the model, and laundering the cycle as objective forecasting.
cases.harm: a feedback loop that hard-codes historical over-policing of Black and brown neighborhoods into 'objective' forecasts
cases.preciseName: a recursive automation of past disparate enforcement — the model recommends patrols where past arrests were concentrated, generating new arrests that train the next iteration
cases.citation: Brennan Center for Justice (2021). 'Predictive Policing Explained.'

cases.decision: Let advertiser bids and click-through rates jointly determine which ad text appeared next to which name — with no audit on race-coded query terms.
cases.outcome: Searching a Black-coded name was up to 25% more likely to surface an arrest-record ad than searching a white-coded name. Suspicion was pre-loaded onto Black names at the moment a stranger Googled them.
cases.harm: ad-delivery infrastructure pre-loaded suspicion onto Black names at the moment a stranger Googled them
cases.preciseName: racially differentiated ad delivery — the same query returned criminal-record ads for Black-coded names and neutral ads for white-coded names
cases.citation: Sweeney, L. (2013). 'Discrimination in Online Ad Delivery.' Communications of the ACM.

cases.tool: Mainstream text-to-image models (Midjourney, Stable Diffusion, DALL-E)
cases.decision: Scraped training images from a web that over-represented Indigenous peoples in historical, costumed, and ethnographic contexts.
cases.outcome: Prompts for Indigenous people render historical reenactors by default — present-tense, plural, contemporary Indigenous life is filtered out of the machine-default image of who exists today.
cases.harm: the model treats Indigenous people as a closed historical category rather than ongoing, plural, contemporary peoples
cases.preciseName: temporal erasure — Indigenous identity is rendered as the past, not the present, by default, regardless of the prompt's framing
cases.citation: Birhane, A. et al. (2023). 'Hate Scaling Laws For Data-Swamps.' FAccT.

cases.decision: Default representations of high-status professions were never re-balanced for gender; 'authority' visual cues were learned from a male-dominated image distribution.
cases.outcome: 'A marketing professor' produced a man in a blazer, gravitas, Harvard energy. Adding 'female' downgraded the same prompt to a timid school-teacher visual until the user fought multiple rounds for parity.
cases.preciseName: the system encodes the default professor as masculine and downgrades authority signals when 'female' is added
cases.citation: Kris Krüg, Punk Rock AI / Both Hands Full (May 2026 talk anecdote).

cases.tool: Frontier large language models (GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama)
cases.decision: Built tokenizers and training mixes that over-weight English; pivoted multilingual reasoning through English internal representations.
cases.outcome: Non-English input gets flattened on the way in and on the way out — dialect, idiom, and cultural reference erased — while the product is marketed as multilingual.
cases.harm: English is being treated as the unmarked default of human thought, and every non-English speaker gets the lossy version
cases.preciseName: an English-pivot architecture that flattens dialect, idiom, and cultural reference on the way in and on the way out, while marketing itself as multilingual
cases.citation: Bender, E. M., Gebru, T., McMillan-Major, A., & Shmitchell, S. (2021). 'On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots.' FAccT.

cases.tool: Auto-caption and alt-text generation across major platforms
cases.decision: Built and audited the systems with sighted, hearing engineering teams; treated 'has captions' as the success metric, not 'captions usable by the people who need them.'
cases.outcome: Captions and alt text technically exist — and routinely fail the disabled users they were ostensibly built for. Compliance theater satisfies a checkbox while the people the feature is for stay locked out.
cases.harm: accessibility theater — accommodations that satisfy a checkbox but actively fail the people they're branded as serving
cases.preciseName: the system is built and audited by people who never have to use it, so the failure modes that matter to disabled users never get into the test set
cases.citation: Whittaker, M. et al. (2019). 'Disability, Bias, and AI.' AI Now Institute.
